idioms.idiom: bite off more than you can chew
idioms.definition: To take on more responsibility or work than you are able to manage
examples: He bit off more than he could chew when he agreed to run three projects at once.
Be careful not to bite off more than you can chew with this contract.
She realised she had bitten off more than she could chew after agreeing to organise the whole conference alone.
idioms.usageTip: Often used as a warning or in hindsight. The metaphor comes from eating – taking a bite too large to chew comfortably.

idioms.idiom: between a rock and a hard place
idioms.definition: In a situation where you must choose between two equally unpleasant or difficult options
examples: I'm between a rock and a hard place – if I say yes, I'll disappoint my family, and if I say no, I'll lose the client.
The government is between a rock and a hard place on the housing issue.
He found himself between a rock and a hard place when both job offers had major drawbacks.
idioms.usageTip: Used to describe genuine dilemmas with no easy solution. More dramatic and vivid than simply saying 'in a difficult position'.

idioms.definition: To introduce a complicated, sensitive, or difficult situation that creates more problems than expected
examples: Bringing up the pay disparity really opened a can of worms.
Don't open a can of worms by raising that topic at the board meeting.
The investigation opened a can of worms – it uncovered problems in multiple departments.
idioms.usageTip: Implies that raising an issue will lead to a cascade of complications. Often used as a warning not to bring something up.

idioms.definition: To make a situation more confusing or complicated than it needs to be
examples: His contradictory statements just muddied the waters further.
I don't want to muddy the waters by raising a new concern at this stage.
The conflicting reports have really muddied the waters.
idioms.usageTip: Clear water = clarity. Muddy water = confusion. Use when additional information or actions make a situation harder to understand.

idioms.definition: To endure a difficult or unpleasant situation without complaining, even if you dislike it
examples: The commute is awful, but I just grin and bear it.
She didn't enjoy the conference at all, but she grinned and bore it for the sake of her career.
There's nothing you can do about the delay – just grin and bear it.
idioms.usageTip: Similar to 'bite the bullet', but 'grin and bear it' often involves tolerating something ongoing, while 'bite the bullet' is more about one decisive moment.

idioms.definition: To be mentally alert, focused, and ready to deal with whatever might happen
examples: You'll need to have your wits about you during the negotiation.
Travelling alone in an unfamiliar city means you have to have your wits about you.
She always has her wits about her in a crisis.
idioms.usageTip: Often used as advice before a challenging situation. 'Keep your wits about you' is also common.

fillInExercises.sentence: He agreed to manage all five departments, but I think he's ___.
options: bitten off more than he can chew
between a rock and a hard place
fillInExercises.correct: bitten off more than he can chew
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bite off more than you can chew' means to take on more than you can realistically manage.

fillInExercises.sentence: Raising the topic of historical grievances at the summit really ___.
options: bit off more than they could chew
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Open a can of worms' means to introduce an issue that creates unexpectedly many complications.

fillInExercises.sentence: The two contradictory reports only served to ___ even further.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Muddy the waters' means to make a situation more confusing or complicated.

fillInExercises.sentence: She was ___ – she had to either miss her best friend's wedding or lose the contract.
options: between a rock and a hard place
biting off more than she could chew
fillInExercises.correct: between a rock and a hard place
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Between a rock and a hard place' describes a dilemma with two equally bad options.

fillInExercises.sentence: The noise from the neighbours was unbearable, but we just had to ___ until we could move.
options: bite off more than we could chew
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Grin and bear it' means to tolerate something unpleasant without complaining.

fillInExercises.sentence: When dealing with difficult clients, you need to ___ and stay calm.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Have your wits about you' means to stay mentally alert and focused, ready for challenges.

lines.speaker: Elena
lines.text: I think I've [bitten off more than I can chew|bite off more than you can chew]. I promised to deliver three proposals this week.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: That's ambitious. Are you managing okay?

lines.speaker: Elena
lines.text: Barely. And I'm [between a rock and a hard place|between a rock and a hard place] – if I ask for an extension, the client will be annoyed, but if I rush, the quality will suffer.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Don't [muddy the waters|muddy the waters] by trying to renegotiate too many things at once.

lines.speaker: Elena
lines.text: You're right. I'll just focus on one proposal first.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: And [have your wits about you|have your wits about you] during the client call – they tend to ask very detailed questions.

lines.speaker: Elena
lines.text: I will. I just hope I don't [open a can of worms|open a can of worms] by admitting how stretched I am.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Be strategic. And for the parts you can't change – you might just have to [grin and bear it|grin and bear it].

lines.speaker: CEO
lines.text: The press has got hold of the story. We need to respond today.

lines.speaker: PR Manager
lines.text: We're [between a rock and a hard place|between a rock and a hard place]. Saying too little looks like we're hiding something, but saying too much could [open a can of worms|open a can of worms].

lines.speaker: CEO
lines.text: And the legal team's statement yesterday just [muddied the waters|muddy the waters] further.

lines.speaker: PR Manager
lines.text: Exactly. Everyone needs to [have their wits about them|have your wits about you] today.

lines.speaker: CEO
lines.text: We should have anticipated this. I think we [bit off more than we could chew|bite off more than you can chew] with the rapid expansion.

lines.speaker: PR Manager
lines.text: Perhaps. But we just have to [grin and bear it|grin and bear it] for now and manage the fallout professionally.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.idiom: bite off more than you can chew
pairs.meaning: take on more than you can handle

pairs.idiom: between a rock and a hard place
pairs.meaning: faced with two equally difficult options
introduce an issue causing many complications

quizExercises.sentence: The contradictory witness statements ___ even more than the initial confusion.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Muddy the waters' means to make things more complicated or confusing.

quizExercises.sentence: In an unfamiliar negotiation environment, you need to ___.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Have your wits about you' means to stay focused and mentally sharp, ready for anything.

quizExercises.question: Which situation best illustrates 'opening a can of worms'?
options: A team calmly resolves a minor scheduling conflict
Raising one question about company finances reveals a chain of mismanagement issues
A manager successfully simplifies a complex project
quizExercises.explanation: 'Open a can of worms' means to bring up an issue that then causes many further complications.

quizExercises.idiom: between a rock and a hard place
quizExercises.question: Which scenario best describes being 'between a rock and a hard place'?
options: You have to choose between two jobs and both are equally appealing
You have to choose between resigning or accepting unfair conditions – neither option is good
You are unsure whether to go out or stay home
quizExercises.explanation: 'Between a rock and a hard place' describes a genuine dilemma where both options are equally unpleasant.
